messages.content: how to check burp suite license how many device used

To check the number of devices on which Burp Suite has been activated using a single license, you can follow these instructions:

1. Open Burp Suite on your computer.

2. Go to the "Help" tab in the top navigation bar.

3. From the drop-down menu, select "License information."

4. A window will appear displaying the license details, including the number of "Licensed devices" or "Seats."

5. Note the number of devices listed under the "Licensed devices" or "Seats" section. This indicates the number of devices on which Burp Suite can be activated using the current license.

If you find that the number of devices listed exceeds your expectations or if you suspect any unauthorized usage, you may need to contact the Burp Suite support team or the official distributor to investigate the issue further.
http method allowed prevention in web.confi file

To prevent certain HTTP methods from being used in your web application, you can update your web.config file with the following steps:

1. Locate the web.config file in the root directory of your web application.

2. Open the web.config file using a text editor.

3. Inside the `<system.webServer>` section, add the following code:

```xml
<security>
  <requestFiltering>
    <verbs>
      <remove verb="PUT"/>
      <remove verb="DELETE"/>
      <!-- Add more "remove" tags for any other methods you want to prevent -->
    </verbs>
  </requestFiltering>
</security>
```

4. Save the web.config file.

5. Restart the web server or application pool for the changes to take effect.

With the above configuration, the "PUT" and "DELETE" HTTP methods will be blocked and inaccessible for your web application. You can add more `<remove>` tags inside the `<verbs>` section to prevent other methods as needed.

Note: Keep in mind that some HTTP methods, such as "GET" and "POST," are essential for normal web application functionality, so avoid removing them unless you have a specific requirement.
http header injection prevention in web.confi file

To prevent HTTP header injection in your web application, you can update your web.config file with the following steps:

1. Locate the web.config file in the root directory of your web application.

2. Open the web.config file using a text editor.

3. Inside the `<system.webServer>` section, add the following code:

```xml
<httpProtocol>
  <customHeaders>
    <remove name="X-Powered-By" />
    <!-- Add more "add" tags for any other headers you want to remove or modify -->
  </customHeaders>
</httpProtocol>
```

4. Save the web.config file.

5. Restart the web server or application pool for the changes to take effect.

By using the configuration above, you remove the "X-Powered-By" header, which is often targeted by attackers. You can add more `<add>` tags inside the `<customHeaders>` section to remove or modify other headers known to be vulnerable to injection attacks.

Additionally, ensure your application correctly sanitizes and validates any user-supplied input to prevent injection attacks.
http header injection prevention in web.config file

To prevent HTTP header injection in your web application, you can update your web.config file with the following steps:

1. Locate the web.config file in the root directory of your web application.
2. Open the web.config file using a text editor.
3. Inside the `<system.webServer>` section, add the following code:

```xml
<httpProtocol>
  <customHeaders>
    <remove name="X-Powered-By" />
    <remove name="Server" />
   
    <add name="X-XSS-Protection" value="1; mode=block" />
    <add name="X-Content-Type-Options" value="nosniff" />
    <add name="Strict-Transport-Security" value="max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains" />
    
    <!-- Add more custom headers as needed -->
  </customHeaders>
</httpProtocol>
```

4. Save the web.config file.
5. Restart the web server or application pool for the changes to take effect.

The above configuration implements a set of recommended security headers to mitigate HTTP header injection vulnerabilities. It removes the "X-Powered-By" and "Server" headers, which can provide attackers information about the backend server.

It also adds the following security headers for protections:

- "X-XSS-Protection": Enables the browser's built-in Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) protection.
- "X-Content-Type-Options": Prevents the browser from MIME sniffing content types.
- "Strict-Transport-Security": Enforces secure connections by only allowing HTTPS and specifies the duration for which browsers should default to HTTPS.

Feel free to modify or add more custom headers depending on your specific requirements and recommendations from security guidelines.

Metasploitable is a vulnerable virtual machine intentionally designed for penetration testing and exploit development practice. It is a pre-configured image containing a range of vulnerable applications and services. Metasploitable is created by the team behind the Metasploit Framework, a popular open-source penetration testing tool.

The purpose of Metasploitable is to provide a target environment on which security professionals and ethical hackers can test and develop their skills in a legal and controlled setting. By exploiting the vulnerabilities present in Metasploitable, users can learn about common vulnerabilities, practice exploitation techniques, and study defensive strategies to improve the security of real-world applications and systems.

Using Metasploitable, security professionals can familiarize themselves with common attack techniques and develop effective countermeasures, helping organizations improve their overall security posture. It is important to note that Metasploitable should only be used in a legal and authorized manner for educational and testing purposes.
